summary: American Abstract Expressionist painter (1915–1991) best known for the Elegy to the Spanish Republic series — over 100 paintings of black ovals and rectangular bars on white backgrounds inspired by the Spanish Civil War and Lorca's poem 'At Five in the Afternoon.' Also created the Open series, Je t'aime series, and the painting 'Drunk with Turpentine.' Member of the New York School; co-founded the Fine Arts Work Center in Provincetown with Stanley Kunitz; co-edited the journal Possibilities with Harold Rosenberg; edited Documents of Modern Art. Husband of Helen Frankenthaler. Mentored by Roberto Matta in automatism; encouraged by critic Meyer Schapiro.

works.name: Elegy to the Spanish Republic (series)
works.description: Over 100 paintings featuring alternating black ovals and rectangular bars on white backgrounds, inspired by the Spanish Civil War. The most famous entries are the 34th, 108th, and 110th. The series grew out of a 1948 drawing for Harold Rosenberg's poem in the magazine Possibilities, and was inspired by André Malraux's speech at a San Francisco rally. First entry titled At Five in the Afternoon (from a Lorca poem); title was changed because New Yorkers associated the original name with cocktails. Motherwell related the shapes to a dead bull's testicles, and called the series 'a private insistence that a terrible death happened that should not be forgot.' He also described it as 'a funeral for something one cared about.' He sometimes painted over works in the series with new colors, leaving the original as underpainting. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Robert Motherwell (1915–1991) was an American Abstract Expressionist painter and a core member of the New York School. He is best known for the Elegy to the Spanish Republic series — over 100 paintings featuring alternating black ovals and rectangular bars on white backgrounds inspired by the Spanish Civil War. The series grew out of a 1948 drawing for Harold Rosenberg's poem published in the magazine Possibilities, and was also inspired by André Malraux's speech at a San Francisco rally. The first entry was titled At Five in the Afternoon, from a Federico Garcia Lorca poem; Motherwell changed the original title because New Yorkers associated it with cocktails. He described the series as 'a funeral for something one cared about' and 'a private insistence that a terrible death happened that should not be forgot.' He related the recurring oval shapes to a dead bull's testicles, and compared himself to a child painting a flower bouquet who, like Cézanne, returns to essential forms. The most famous individual entries are the 34th, 108th, and 110th. Motherwell sometimes painted over works in the series, leaving original colors as underpainting.

Beyond the Elegy series, Motherwell created the nearly monochromatic Open series and the Je t'aime series incorporating cursive French text. He painted Drunk with Turpentine, a work of thick black brushstrokes with faint orange halos. He also made Mexican Sketchbook, inspired by a 1941 boat trip to Mexico with Roberto Matta, who taught him automatism technique on that trip. He produced abstract works inspired by poetry, including an abstract depiction of Mallarmé's Swan and The Voyage, from Baudelaire.

Motherwell edited the Documents of Modern Art series and co-edited the art journal Possibilities with Harold Rosenberg. His anthology Dada Poets and Painters helped inspire the Fluxus movement. He co-founded the Fine Arts Work Center in Provincetown, Massachusetts with poet Stanley Kunitz. His third wife was abstract expressionist Helen Frankenthaler, nicknamed 'the golden couple.' He was encouraged by Marxist critic Meyer Schapiro, who wrote 'From Mozarabic to Romanesque in Silos.'
